lineage: The Book of Pastoral Rule, and Selected Epistles, of Gregory the Great.
Of the Life of the Pastor.
That the ruler should not set his heart on pleasing men, and yet should give heed to what ought to please them.

metadata.author: The Book of Pastoral Rule, and Selected Epistles, of Gregory the Great

ccel.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

source.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

title: That the ruler should not set his heart on pleasing men, and yet should give heed to what ought to please them.

source.title: NPNF-212. Leo the Great, Gregory the Great

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: Meanwhile it is also necessary for the ruler to keep wary watch, lest the lust of pleasing men assail him; lest, when he studiously penetrates the things that are within, and providently supplies the things that are without, he seek to be beloved of those that are under him more than truth; lest, while, supported by his good deeds, he seems not to belong to the world, self-love estrange him from his Maker.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: For he is the Redeemer’s enemy who through the good works which he does covets being loved by the Church instead of Him; since a servant whom the bridegroom has sent with gifts to the bride is guilty of treacherous thought if he desires to please the eyes of the bride.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: And in truth this self-love, when it has got possession of a ruler’s mind, sometimes carries it away inordinately to softness, but sometimes to roughness.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: For from love of himself the ruler’s mind is inclined to softness, because, when he observes those that are under him sinning, he does not presume to reprove them, lest their affection for himself should grow dull; nay sometimes he smooths down with flatteries the offence of his subordinates which he ought to have rebuked.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: Hence it is well said through the prophet, Woe unto them that sew cushions under every elbow, and make pillows under the head of every stature to catch souls (Ezek. xiii.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: 18); inasmuch as to put cushions under every elbow is to cherish with bland flatteries souls that are falling from their uprightness and reclining themselves in this world’s enjoyment.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: For it is as though the elbow of a recumbent person rested on a cushion and his head on pillows, when the hardness of reproof is withdrawn from one who sins, and when the softness of favour is offered to him, that he may lie softly in error, while no roughness of contradiction troubles him.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: But so rulers who love themselves undoubtedly shew themselves to those by whom they fear they may be injured in their pursuit of temporal glory.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: Such indeed as they see to have no power against them they ever keep down with roughness of rigid censure, never admonish them gently, but, forgetful of pastoral kindness, terrify them with the rights of domination.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: Such the divine voice rightly upbraids through the prophet, saying, But with austerity and power did ye rule them (Ezek. xxiv.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: For, loving themselves more than their Maker, they lift up themselves haughtily towards those that are under them, considering not what they ought to do, but what they can do; they have no fear of future judgment; they glory insolently in temporal power; it pleases them to be free to do even unlawful things, and that no one among their subordinates should contradict them.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: He, then, who sets his mind on doing wrong things, and yet wishes all other men to hold their peace about them, is himself a witness to himself that he desires to be loved himself more than the truth, which he is unwilling should be defended against him.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: There is indeed no one who so lives as not to some extent to fail in duty.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: He, then, desires the truth to be loved more fully than himself, who wishes to be spared by no one against the truth.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: For hence Peter willingly accepted Paul’s rebuke (Galat. ii.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: 11); hence David humbly listened to the reproof of his subject (2 Sam. xii.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: 7); because good rulers, being themselves unconscious of loving with partial affection, believe the word of free sincerity from subjects to be the homage of humility.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: But meanwhile it is necessary that the care of government be tempered with so great skill of management that the mind of subjects, when it has become able to feel rightly on some subjects, should so advance to liberty of speech that liberty still break not out into pride; lest, while liberty of the tongue is perchance conceded to them overmuch, the humility of their life be lost.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: It is to be borne in mind also, that it is right for good rulers to desire to please men; but this in order to draw their neighbours by the sweetness of their own character to affection for the truth; not that they should long to be themselves loved, but should make affection for themselves as a sort of road by which to lead the hearts of their hearers to the love of the Creator.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: For it is indeed difficult for a preacher who is not loved, however well he may preach, to be willingly listened to.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: He, then, who is over others ought to study to be loved to the end that he may be listened to, and still not seek love for its own sake, lest he be found in the hidden usurpation of his thought to rebel against Him whom in his office he appears to serve.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: Which thing Paul insinuates well, when, manifesting the secret of his affection for us, he says, Even as I please all men in all things (1 Cor. x.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: And yet he says again, If I yet pleased men, I should not be the servant of Christ (Gal. i.

verses.book: Of the Life of the Pastor.
verses.text: Thus Paul pleases, and pleases not; because in that he desires to please he seeks that not he himself should please men, but truth through him.
